longDescription:
The Blood of Isis, Amulet of Protection and Resurrection The Tyet, also known as the Knot of Isis or the Blood of Isis, is an ancient Egyptian
                    amulet closely associated with the goddess Isis and her powers of protection, healing,
                    and resurrection. Resembling the ankh but with its arms folded down, the tyet was one
                    of the most important funerary amulets in ancient Egypt.
name: Tyet (Knot of Isis)

sources.name: Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of funerary texts containing spells and rituals to aid the deceased in the afterlife.
sources.title: Book of the Dead

sources.name: Coffin Texts
sources.description: A collection of spells and funerary texts inscribed on coffins during the Middle Kingdom.
sources.title: Coffin Texts

sources.name: Papyrus of Ani
sources.description: An illuminated manuscript version of the Book of the Dead.
sources.title: Papyrus of Ani

sources.name: Plutarch, De Iside et Osiride
sources.description: A Greek philosophical text providing an account of the myths of Isis and Osiris.
sources.title: Plutarch, De Iside et Osiride

sources.name: Andrews, Carol. Amulets of Ancient Egypt.
sources.description: A comprehensive study of Egyptian amulets and their significance.
sources.title: Andrews, Carol. Amulets of Ancient Egypt.

materials: Red jasper, carnelian, or red glass
shortDescription: The Blood of Isis, Amulet of Protection and Resurrection

extendedContent.content:
The Tyet, also known as the Knot of Isis or the Blood of Isis, is an ancient Egyptian
                    amulet closely associated with the goddess Isis and her powers of protection, healing,
                    and resurrection. Resembling the ankh but with its arms folded down, the tyet was one
                    of the most important funerary amulets in ancient Egypt.
                
                
                    The amulet was traditionally made from red materials - jasper, carnelian, or red glass -
                    representing the magical blood of Isis. It was placed on the throat or chest of mummies
                    to invoke the protection of Isis in the journey through the underworld.
extendedContent.title: Description and Appearance

extendedContent.content:
Chapter 156 of the Book of the Dead specifically concerns the tyet amulet, providing
                    instructions for its preparation and the spell to be recited:
                
                
                    "You have your blood, Isis. You have your power, Isis. You have your magic, Isis.
                    The amulet is a protection for this Great One; it will drive away whoever would
                    commit a crime against him."
                
                
                    The text specifies that the amulet should be made of red jasper and placed on the
                    deceased's neck, ensuring that the person would have the protection of Isis's blood
                    and magical power throughout their journey through the Duat (underworld).
extendedContent.title: The Book of the Dead

description: The Blood of Isis, Amulet of Protection and Resurrection

keyMyths.title: Protection in the Afterlife
keyMyths.description: The Tyet amulet, often made of red materials like carnelian or red jasper, was placed on mummies to ensure Isis's protection in the afterlife, safeguarding the deceased from harm and ensuring successful rebirth. Its power was believed to actively invoke the goddess's protective abilities.

keyMyths.title: The Blood of Isis
keyMyths.description: Referred to as the 'Blood of Isis,' the Tyet is linked to the life force and regenerative power of the goddess, symbolizing her ability to heal wounds and restore life, both physically and spiritually. This association underscores its role in resurrection and continued existence.

keyMyths.title: Counteracting Malevolent Forces
keyMyths.description: The Tyet was employed as a powerful countermeasure against malevolent spirits and forces that threatened the deceased's journey through the underworld, creating a protective barrier against demonic entities and ensuring safe passage to the realm of Osiris. It acted as a ward against chaos and disruption.

keyMyths.title: Association with Osiris's Resurrection
keyMyths.description: Connected to the myth of Osiris's resurrection, the Tyet amulet, alongside the Djed pillar, embodies Isis's role in restoring her husband to life, symbolizing the triumph over death and the cyclical nature of existence. The amulet represents her magical abilities and unwavering devotion.

keyMyths.title: Royal Protection and Divine Authority
keyMyths.description: Worn by pharaohs and royalty, the Tyet amulet reinforced their divine authority and connection to Isis, signifying their right to rule and their protection under the goddess's patronage. It served as a symbol of legitimacy and divine favor, ensuring the stability of the kingdom.
keyMyths.source: Statues and depictions of pharaohs wearing the Tyet amulet
